```

This crash doesn’t happen every time. Based on the stack trace, it only crashes when running the benchmark. The benchmark uses `for b.Loop() {`, so it seems quite likely that it’s related to compiler instrumenting.

cc @JunyangShao @randall77 


### What did you expect to see?

no crash

comment.body:
I got a core dump, and I used `gdb` and `info symbol` to figure out that the address which has the bad pointer is the `os.lstat` variable (https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/master:src/os/file.go;l=432;drc=0451816430486a25966dd76aa92735ac0588b8e5). I don't yet know how plausible that is, but it makes sense that the syscall tests would use the `os` package and random memory corruption could hit that variable.

But also, something about this doesn't seem quite right to me...

issue.title: cmd/compile: miscompile of global array initialization

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: Critical

This crash doesn’t happen every time. Based on the stack trace, it only crashes when running the benchmark. The benchmark uses `for b.Loop() {`, so it seems quite likely that it’s related to compiler instrumenting.

cc @JunyangShao @randall77 


### What did you expect to see?

no crash

comment.body: Based on the address, the bad pointer is consistently found in a low address, suggesting that some global variable contains the bad pointer.

issue.title: cmd/compile: miscompile of global array initialization

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: Critical
labels.description: A critical problem that affects the availability or correctness of production systems built using Go

This crash doesn’t happen every time. Based on the stack trace, it only crashes when running the benchmark. The benchmark uses `for b.Loop() {`, so it seems quite likely that it’s related to compiler instrumenting.

cc @JunyangShao @randall77 


### What did you expect to see?

no crash

comment.body: Another data point: if it fails, it seems to always seems to fail before any benchmark output is produced. I can also get it to fail much more reliably under some stress testing tool.

```

This crash doesn’t happen every time. Based on the stack trace, it only crashes when running the benchmark. The benchmark uses `for b.Loop() {`, so it seems quite likely that it’s related to compiler instrumenting.

cc @JunyangShao @randall77 


### What did you expect to see?

no crash

comment.body:
Perhaps unsurprisingly, the bisection narrows down to https://go.googlesource.com/go/+/836fa745188fddf49070d010161e30f360862e57, which introduced the benchmark, and also optimized `Clearenv` in the first place.

CC @randall77 @cyphar

issue.title: cmd/compile: miscompile of global array initialization

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: Critical
labels.description: A critical problem that affects the availability or correctness of production systems built using Go

comment.body:
It's not clear to me that this would be a runtime bug. We've only excluded `b.Loop`, which is useful, but it could still be a compiler liveness bug. (Could be a runtime bug too, or it could be a bug in the syscall tests!)

Furthermore, the failure only reproduces for me if you actually run the tests, as OP noted above. That suggests the bug is being triggered by the tests, not the benchmark anyway. Could be related to cleanups, could be a latent bug in the syscall tests exposed by another CL, could be a runtime bug.

I don't think we know. I'll run a bisection too, since @JunyangShao reported that his was inconclusive.

Here's the script I'm running at each bisect step:

```
cd $GO/src && ./make.bash && cd syscall && go test -c . && GOMAXPROCS=4 stress2 -max-fails=1 -max-passes=1000 -fail='found bad pointer' ./syscall.test -test.bench=.
```

issue.title: x/build: LUCI outage: `cas download` step fails (infra failure) in builders

labels.name: Builders
labels.description: x/build issues (builders, bots, dashboards)

labels.name: Soon
labels.description: This needs action soon. (recent regressions, service outages, unusual time-sensitive situations)

milestone.title: Unreleased
milestone.description: Issues that do not affect released Go code and binaries.

issue.body:
First noticed on some Gerrit changes in presubmit.

Example failures:
- https://ci.chromium.org/ui/p/golang/builders/try/gotip-linux-386/b8692676113130842481/overview
- https://ci.chromium.org/ui/p/golang/builders/try/gotip-linux-amd64/b8692675920058132961/overview
- https://ci.chromium.org/ui/p/golang/builders/try/gotip-linux-arm64/b8692676420959567137/overview

`cas` output looks like:
```
goroutine 1:
#0 go.chromium.org/luci/client/cmd/cas/casimpl/download.go:515 - casimpl.(*downloadRun).doDownload()
  reason: failed to download files

#1 go.chromium.org/luci/client/cmd/cas/casimpl/download.go:574 - casimpl.(*downloadRun).Run()
#2 github.com/maruel/subcommands@v1.1.1/subcommands.go:395 - subcommands.Run()

`cas` output looks like:

#2 github.com/maruel/subcommands@v1.1.1/subcommands.go:395 - subcommands.Run()
#3 cas/main.go:95 - main.main()
#4 runtime/proc.go:271 - runtime.main()
#5 runtime/asm_arm64.s:1222 - runtime.goexit()
```

We're currently trying to figure out the problem and mitigate the issue.

issue.title: Delayed update
issue.body:
https://github.com/ustclug/discussions/issues/517

Design:

- `--delay-update-metadata`: Delaying known metadata files (deb/yum) till all downloads are done.
- `--delay-update`: Delaying all files till all downloads are done.

issue.title: freebsd-pkg：部分软件包的校验和与上游不符合
issue.body:
### 问题描述 / Bug description

根据 <https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/freebsd-pkg/FreeBSD%3A14%3Aamd64/latest/packagesite.txz>、<https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/freebsd-pkg/FreeBSD%3A14%3Aamd64/latest/data.txz>，以及实际在脚本中起判断作用的 <https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/freebsd-pkg/FreeBSD%3A14%3Aamd64/latest/packagesite.txz>：

```ymal
{"name":"openjdk17","origin":"java/openjdk17","version":"17.0.16+8.1_4","comment":"Java Development Kit 17","maintainer":"java@FreeBSD.org","www":"https://openjdk.java.net/projects/jdk/17/","abi":"FreeBSD:14:amd64","arch":"freebsd:14:x86:64","prefix":"/usr/local","sum":"bbe335d05edebbbebe3d81a97053b25022c73f20980e033937fc2360346aec4f","flatsize":322603597,"path":"All/openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg","repopath":"All/openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg","licenselogic":"single","licenses":["GPLv2"],"pkgsize":165893853,"desc":"An open-source implementation of the Java Platform, Standard Edition,"
```
openjdk17 的 sha256 应为 `bbe335d05edebbbebe3d81a97053b25022c73f20980e033937fc2360346aec4f`，但是下载的  <https://mirrors.ustc.edu.cn/freebsd-pkg/FreeBSD%3A14%3Aamd64/latest/All/openjdk17-17.0.16%2B8.1_4.pkg> 校验和实为 `fbb69747ad18a7451d23278d33b91cf6f3de9b94ca87d0b16d2ca9aadbcfc9f0`

同时，使用官方源 pkg.freebsd.org 获取的 openjdk17 可以正常安装，其校验和同为  `bbe335d05edebbbebe3d81a97053b25022c73f20980e033937fc2360346aec4f`：

```sh
# sha256 /var/cache/pkg/openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg 
SHA256 (/var/cache/pkg/openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg) = bbe335d05edebbbebe3d81a97053b25022c73f20980e033937fc2360346aec4f
```

---

当前使用 USTC 报错如下：

```sh
root@ykla:/usr/ports/aur # pkg ins openjdk17
Updating FreeBSD repository catalogue...
FreeBSD repository is up to date.
Updating FreeBSD-kmods repository catalogue...
FreeBSD-kmods repository is up to date.
All repositories are up to date.
The following 2 package(s) will be affected (of 0 checked):

New packages to be INSTALLED:
        javavmwrapper: 2.7.10 [FreeBSD]
        openjdk17: 17.0.16+8.1_4 [FreeBSD]

Number of packages to be installed: 2

The process will require 308 MiB more space.
158 MiB to be downloaded.

Proceed with this action? [y/N]: y
[1/2] Fetching openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg: 100%  158 MiB  41.5MB/s    00:04    
pkg: cached package openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4: missing or size mismatch, fetching from remote
[2/2] Fetching openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg: 100%  158 MiB  55.3MB/s    00:03    
pkg: cached package openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4: missing or size mismatch, cannot continue
Consider running 'pkg update -f'
Fetching openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg: 100%  158 MiB  55.3MB/s    00:03    
pkg: cached package openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4: missing or size mismatch, fetching from remote
Fetching openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4.pkg: 100%  158 MiB  55.3MB/s    00:03    
pkg: cached package openjdk17-17.0.16+8.1_4: missing or size mismatch, cannot continue
Consider running 'pkg update -f'
```

此问题之前频繁发生，但我从未重视过。今天中午社区有人反馈，我刚才又复现了一遍。

issue.title: feat: support PEP 658 and 714
issue.body: Closes: https://github.com/taoky/shadowmire/issues/12

comment.body:
> > > > 有一个问题，当前的版本跟踪是按包跟踪的，而不是按文件跟踪的，这导致程序无法单独跟踪 metadata 的状态。即使可以跟踪 metadata 的状态，PyPI 也没法单独跟踪 metadata 的变更。
> > 
> > > > 我考虑以下的解决方案：
> > 
> > > > 
> > 
> > > > * 提供一个可以手动运行的指令，同步所有包文件的 metadata
> > 
> > > > * 对于尚未同步 metadata 的部分，Simple API 的 HTML/JSON 文件去掉 metadata 相关信息
> > 
> > > 
> > 
> > > 我看了一下代码，可以修改 skip_this_package 的实现吧。
> > 
> > 
> > 
> > 对于还没有更新到最新版本的镜像是可以的。但已经更新的镜像已对 Simple API 的 HTML/JSON 文件进行了修改，在相关的包有下一次更新前依然会保持在缺少 metadata 的状态。
> 
> 嗯，这些包只能 verify 或者手动 do_update

（我的考虑是目前的损坏是一个短期的状态，未来版本的 shadowmire 不会出现缺少 metadata 的状态，所以把会导致缺少的问题修好就足够了；已经出现问题的包也有办法处理）

release.name: Lotus v1.0
release.body:
**Release Date**: January 28, 2026

We are excited to announce the first stable release of **Lotus v1.0**, a comprehensive program analysis, verification, and optimization framework built on LLVM. This release represents a major milestone with a mature, production-ready codebase that has been validated through multiple research publications and industrial applications.

## Overview

Lotus v1.0 provides a versatile and industrial-scale program analysis framework that combines multiple analysis techniques to detect security vulnerabilities, concurrency bugs, and numerical errors in C/C++ programs. The framework has been tested on x86/ARM Linux and ARM Mac using LLVM-14 with Z3.

## Major Features

### 🐛 Comprehensive Bug Detection Framework

Lotus v1.0 includes a unified bug detection framework with four major checker categories:

#### 1. **Pulse Checker** (NEW in v1.0)
- **Biabductive Analysis**: Path-sensitive interprocedural analysis inspired by Infer's Pulse
- **Memory Safety Detection**: 
  - Use-after-free (CWE-416)
  - Null pointer dereference (CWE-476)
  - Uninitialized read (CWE-457)
- **Advanced Features**:
  - Disjunctive domain for path-sensitive reasoning
  - Loop abstraction with widening and invariant inference
  - Function summaries for scalable interprocedural analysis
  - Taint analysis integration for security vulnerabilities
  - Latent issue tracking

#### 2. **GVFA (Global Value Flow Analysis) Checkers**
- Null pointer dereference detection
- Use-after-free detection
- Uninitialized variable use detection
- Invalid free() detection
- Stack address misuse detection

#### 3. **KINT Numerical Checkers**
- Integer overflow detection (signed and unsigned)
- Division by zero detection
- Bad shift detection
- Array out-of-bounds detection
- Dead branch detection
- Range analysis with SMT solving

#### 4. **Concurrency Checkers**
- Data race detection using MHP (May Happen in Parallel) analysis
- Deadlock detection using lock set analysis
- Atomicity violation detection
- Enhanced thread handling and static vector clock analysis

### 🔍 Advanced Alias Analysis

Lotus provides multiple state-of-the-art alias analysis algorithms:

| Analysis | Tool/Command | Analysis Type | Flow-Sensitive | Context-Sensitive | Field-Sensitive | Notes |
|----------|--------------|---------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-------|
| **SparrowAA** | `sparrow-aa` | Inclusion-based | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (CI, 1-CFA, 2-CFA) | ❌ No | More graph simplification algorithms; no on-the-fly callgraph construction |
| **AserPTA** | `aser-aa` | Inclusion-based | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (CI, 1-CFA, 2-CFA, Origin) | ✅ Yes | On-the-fly callgraph construction; supports both field-insensitive and field-sensitive modes |
| **CFLAA** | - | - | ❌ No | ❌ No | - | From LLVM |
| **DyckAA** | - | Unification-based | ❌ No | ❌ No | - | - |
| **Dynamic** | - | Dynamic | - | - | - | Runtime analysis |
| **FPA** | `fpa` | Type-based | - | - | - | Function pointer analysis |
| **LotusAA** | `lotus-aa` | Inclusion-based | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | - | Flow- and context-sensitive |
| **seadsa** | `sea-dsa-dg`, `seadsa-tool` | Unification-based | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | - | Context-sensitive heap (heap cloning) |
| **SRAA** | - | Range-based | ❌ No | ❌ No | - | Flow- and context-insensitive |
| **UnderApproxAA** | - | Pattern-based | - | - | - | Must-alias analysis |
| **AllocAA** | - | - | - | - | - | - |
| **TPA** | `tpa` | Inclusion-based | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (k-limiting) | - | Flow- and context-sensitive with k-limiting |
### 📊 Intermediate Representations

- **PDG (Program Dependence Graph)**: Comprehensive program representation with Cypher query support
- **SVFG (Sparse Value Flow Graph)**: Efficient value flow representation
- **DyckVFG**: Context-sensitive value flow graph
- **Thin Slicing**: Advanced slicing capabilities based on EECS-2006-184

### 🧮 Constraint Solving & Data Flow

- **SMT Solving**: Z3 integration for path-sensitive verification
- **BDD (CUDD)**: Binary decision diagrams for efficient constraint solving
- **WPDS**: Weighted pushdown systems for interprocedural analysis
- **IFDS/IDE Framework**: Interprocedural finite distributive subset / interprocedural distributive environment framework
- **Taint Analysis**: Information flow tracking for security vulnerabilities

### 🔧 Verification & Optimization

- **CLAM**: Abstract interpretation-based verification
- **Seahorn**: Model checking and verification
- **Symbolic Abstraction**: Symbolic execution capabilities
- **Failure-Directed Trimming**: LLVM pass for failure-directed program trimming
- **Loop Invariant Analysis**: Automatic loop invariant inference
- **Function Invariant Analysis**: Function-level invariant analysis

### 📝 Enhanced Reporting & Integration

- **SARIF 2.1.0 Support**: Standard format for static analysis results
- **JSON Output**: Machine-readable bug reports
- **Bug Suppression Management**: Flexible suppression system for known issues
- **Centralized Bug Reporting**: Unified `BugReportMgr` for all checkers
- **Rich Diagnostics**: Detailed traces and source location information
- **CWE Mapping**: Common Weakness Enumeration mappings for all bug types

## Command-Line Tools

Lotus v1.0 provides a comprehensive set of command-line tools:

### Bug Detection Tools
- `lotus-pulse` - Pulse biabductive analysis checker
- `lotus-gvfa` - Global value flow analysis vulnerability checker
- `lotus-kint` - Integer and numerical bug detection
- `lotus-concur` - Concurrency bug detection
- `lotus-taint` - Taint analysis tool

### Alias Analysis Tools
- `lotus-aa` - flow- and context-sensitive analysis
- `dyck-aa` - Dyck-CFL reachability alias analysis
- `aser-aa` - Aser points-to analysis
- `sparrow-aa` - Sparrow points-to analysis
- `fpa` - Function pointer analysis
- `sea-dsa-dg` - Sea-DSA analysis
- `dynaa` - Dynamic alias analysis validation
-  etc.

### Analysis & Query Tools
- `pdg-query` - PDG querying with Cypher support
- `lotus-opt` - Optimization passes
- `clam` - Abstract interpretation verification
- `seahorn` - Model checking

### Solver Tools
- `owl` - SMT solver interface
- `slot` - Constraint solving
- `staub` - Additional solver utilities

## Platform Support

- **Operating Systems**: x86 Linux, ARM Linux, ARM Mac (Apple Silicon)
- **LLVM**: 14.x
- **CMake**: 3.10+
- **Compiler**: C++14 compatible compiler

## Documentation

Comprehensive documentation is available at:
- **Online Docs**: https://zju-pl.github.io/lotus
- **User Guide**: Installation, tutorials, architecture overview
- **API Reference**: Complete developer documentation
- **Examples**: Extensive example code and benchmarks

## Research Impact

Lotus has been used in multiple high-impact research publications:

- **TOSEM 2026**: Compiler Optimizations-Based SMT Simplifications
- **ISSTA 2025**: Program Analysis Combining Generalized Bit-Level and Word-Level Abstractions
- **S&P 2024**: Titan: Efficient Multi-target Directed Greybox Fuzzing
- **USENIX Security 2024**: Type-Based Call Graph Construction with Regional Pointer Information
- **TSE 2024**: Fast and Precise Static Null Exception Analysis
- **OOPSLA 2022**: Indexing the Extended Dyck-CFL Reachability

## Installation

```bash
git clone https://github.com/ZJU-PL/lotus
cd lotus
mkdir build && cd build
cmake ..
make -j$(nproc)
```

For custom LLVM installations:
```bash
cmake .. -DLLVM_BUILD_PATH=/path/to/llvm/lib/cmake/llvm
```

## Quick Start

### Detect Memory Safety Bugs
```bash
# Using Pulse checker
./build/bin/lotus-pulse program.bc

# Using GVFA checker
./build/bin/lotus-gvfa --vuln-type=nullpointer program.bc
```

### Detect Integer Bugs
```bash
./build/bin/lotus-kint --check-all program.bc
```

### Detect Concurrency Bugs
```bash
./build/bin/lotus-concur --check-data-races --check-deadlocks program.bc
```

### Generate SARIF Report
```bash
./build/bin/lotus-pulse --sarif-output=report.sarif program.bc
```

## What's New in v1.0

### Major Additions
- **Pulse Checker**: Complete biabductive analysis framework for memory safety
- **SARIF Support**: Industry-standard reporting format (SARIF 2.1.0)
- **Bug Suppression System**: Flexible suppression management
- **Cypher Query Support**: Query PDG using Cypher language
- **Loop/Function Invariant Analysis**: Automatic invariant inference
- **Failure-Directed Trimming**: LLVM pass for program trimming
- **Enhanced Concurrency Analysis**: Improved MHP and lock set analysis

### Improvements
- Unified bug reporting system across all checkers
- Enhanced diagnostic messages with detailed traces
- Better integration between analysis components
- Improved documentation and examples
- Performance optimizations across multiple components

## Breaking Changes

None. This is the first stable release.

## Known Limitations

- LLVM 14.x is required (automatic download not yet implemented)
- Z3 4.11 is required (automatic download not yet implemented)
- Some advanced features may require additional configuration

## Contributors

We thank all contributors who made this release possible:

- rainoftime / cutelimination
- qingkaishi
- rhuab
- Zahrinas
- Rexxar-Jack-Remar


## Support & Community

- **GitHub**: https://github.com/ZJU-PL/lotus
- **Documentation**: https://zju-pl.github.io/lotus
- **Issues**: Please report bugs and feature requests on GitHub

## Future Roadmap

- Support for additional LLVM versions
- Automatic dependency management
- Enhanced performance optimizations
- Additional bug detection capabilities
- Improved IDE integration

---

**Thank you for using Lotus!** We hope this framework helps advance your program analysis research and development.

## What's Changed
* Fix build by uncommenting necessary include for "Verification/SymbolicAbstraction/Utils/Z3APIExtension.h" by @Rexxar-Jack-Remar in https://github.com/ZJU-PL/lotus/pull/2
* add a condition for clam in lib/Verification/CMakeLists.txt by @Rexxar-Jack-Remar in https://github.com/ZJU-PL/lotus/pull/3

## New Contributors
* @Rexxar-Jack-Remar made their first contribution in https://github.com/ZJU-PL/lotus/pull/2

**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/ZJU-PL/lotus/commits/1.0

issue.title: [Feature][P1]: Add OCI Annotations to container images

labels.name: feature request
labels.description: New feature or request

issue.body:
### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch

Currently vllm images use the vanilla labels from NVIDIA base images:

```
{"maintainer":"NVIDIA CORPORATION <cudatools@nvidia.com>","org.opencontainers.image.ref.name":"ubuntu","org.opencontainers.image.version":"22.04"}
```

We should add more [OCI annotations](https://specs.opencontainers.org/image-spec/annotations/) to the images as metadata. For example, the project name, version / commit hash used to build, and more. And we should override the maintainer name.

There is some similar work in #30593: https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/30953/changes#diff-d4fc32feaf69e41815eaa8608a1687525d03888f9dc68d35c2ac96453cf6028eR217-R239. We should do it in a unified and consistent way.


### Alternatives

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

### Before submitting a new issue...

- [x] Make sure you already searched for relevant issues, and asked the chatbot living at the bottom right corner of the [documentation page](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/), which can answer lots of frequently asked questions.

issue.title: [CI/Build][Docker] Add centralized version manifest for Docker builds

labels.name: ready
labels.description: ONLY add when PR is ready to merge/full CI is needed

issue.body:
## Purpose

Introduces `docker/versions.json` as a centralized, machine-readable version manifest for all pinned dependencies in Docker builds.

## Problem

Version information is currently scattered across `docker/Dockerfile` and various install scripts, making it hard to:
- Track version changes across releases
- Programmatically query versions for CI/tooling
- Maintain consistency

## Solution

**Dockerfile = Source of Truth** with auto-generated bake-native `versions.json`.

## Integration with Docker Bake (#31477)

This PR complements the `docker buildx bake` work in #31477:

| This PR | Bake PR #31477 |
|---------|----------------|
| `versions.json` = version data | `docker-bake.hcl` = build config |
| What versions to use | How to build |

### Bake-Native JSON Format

```json
{
  "variable": {
    "CUDA_VERSION": { "default": "12.9.1" },
    "PYTHON_VERSION": { "default": "3.12" },
    "torch_cuda_arch_list": { "default": "7.0 7.5 8.0 8.9 9.0 10.0 12.0" },
    "FLASHINFER_VERSION": { "default": "0.5.3" },
    "DEEPGEMM_GIT_REF": { "default": "594953acce41793ae00a1233eb516044d604bcb6" },
    ...
  }
}
```

Bake reads this directly - no preprocessing needed:
```bash
docker buildx bake -f docker/docker-bake.hcl -f docker/versions.json
```

### Auto-Generation

`tools/generate_versions_json.py` parses Dockerfile ARG defaults and generates bake-native versions.json:

```bash
python tools/generate_versions_json.py        # Generate
python tools/generate_versions_json.py --check  # CI validation
```

## Usage

```bash
# Simple docker build (uses Dockerfile defaults)
docker build -f docker/Dockerfile .

# Bake with versions.json
docker buildx bake -f docker/docker-bake.hcl -f docker/versions.json

# Override specific versions
docker buildx bake --set "*.args.CUDA_VERSION=12.8"

# Extract versions programmatically (e.g., for downstream packaging)
jq -r '.variable.CUDA_VERSION.default' docker/versions.json
```

## Benefits

1. **Single Source of Truth**: Dockerfile ARG defaults are authoritative
2. **Bake-Native**: versions.json works directly with `docker buildx bake`
3. **Machine-Readable**: Easy to parse with `jq` for CI and external tooling  
4. **Extension Commits Centralized**: DEEPGEMM_GIT_REF, PPLX_COMMIT_HASH, DEEPEP_COMMIT_HASH moved to Dockerfile
5. **Cleaner Diffs**: Version bumps show as clear changes
6. **Low Barrier**: Contributors can still use `docker build .` without learning bake

## Files Changed

- `docker/versions.json` - Bake-native version manifest (auto-generated)
- `docker/Dockerfile` - Source of truth for version defaults
- `tools/generate_versions_json.py` - Auto-generation script with --check mode

## Test Plan

- [x] JSON is valid and parseable by bake
- [x] `docker buildx bake --print` shows correct variable values
- [x] `python tools/generate_versions_json.py --check` passes
- [x] Pre-commit checks pass

comment.body:
> Hi @Harry-Chen, thanks for all the help, and thanks @ywang96 and everyone for the reviews!
> 
> Yes, I think this would be very beneficial. Looking at the release pipeline, as far I can see (please correct me if I'm wrong) the pattern that bake + versions.json addresses - there are 12+ docker build commands with long, and duplicated (across jobs?) `--build-arg` chains where version inconsistencies can easily creep

Yes this is the main concern. Please note we build both wheels and docker images in the release pipeline, and they use different target images.

> I can come up with a proposal/PR next week covering:
>

> 
> These will be the things to still figure out

Sounds good. Feel free to ping me when you have any updates and/or discussions.

issue.title: [CI/Build][Docker] Add centralized version manifest for Docker builds

labels.name: ready
labels.description: ONLY add when PR is ready to merge/full CI is needed

issue.body:
## Purpose

Introduces `docker/versions.json` as a centralized, machine-readable version manifest for all pinned dependencies in Docker builds.

## Problem

Version information is currently scattered across `docker/Dockerfile` and various install scripts, making it hard to:
- Track version changes across releases
- Programmatically query versions for CI/tooling
- Maintain consistency

## Solution

**Dockerfile = Source of Truth** with auto-generated bake-native `versions.json`.

## Integration with Docker Bake (#31477)

This PR complements the `docker buildx bake` work in #31477:

| This PR | Bake PR #31477 |
|---------|----------------|
| `versions.json` = version data | `docker-bake.hcl` = build config |
| What versions to use | How to build |

### Bake-Native JSON Format

```json
{
  "variable": {
    "CUDA_VERSION": { "default": "12.9.1" },
    "PYTHON_VERSION": { "default": "3.12" },
    "torch_cuda_arch_list": { "default": "7.0 7.5 8.0 8.9 9.0 10.0 12.0" },
    "FLASHINFER_VERSION": { "default": "0.5.3" },
    "DEEPGEMM_GIT_REF": { "default": "594953acce41793ae00a1233eb516044d604bcb6" },
    ...
  }
}
```

Bake reads this directly - no preprocessing needed:
```bash
docker buildx bake -f docker/docker-bake.hcl -f docker/versions.json
```

### Auto-Generation

`tools/generate_versions_json.py` parses Dockerfile ARG defaults and generates bake-native versions.json:

```bash
python tools/generate_versions_json.py        # Generate
python tools/generate_versions_json.py --check  # CI validation
```

## Usage

```bash
# Simple docker build (uses Dockerfile defaults)
docker build -f docker/Dockerfile .

# Bake with versions.json
docker buildx bake -f docker/docker-bake.hcl -f docker/versions.json

# Override specific versions
docker buildx bake --set "*.args.CUDA_VERSION=12.8"

# Extract versions programmatically (e.g., for downstream packaging)
jq -r '.variable.CUDA_VERSION.default' docker/versions.json
```

## Benefits

1. **Single Source of Truth**: Dockerfile ARG defaults are authoritative
2. **Bake-Native**: versions.json works directly with `docker buildx bake`
3. **Machine-Readable**: Easy to parse with `jq` for CI and external tooling  
4. **Extension Commits Centralized**: DEEPGEMM_GIT_REF, PPLX_COMMIT_HASH, DEEPEP_COMMIT_HASH moved to Dockerfile
5. **Cleaner Diffs**: Version bumps show as clear changes
6. **Low Barrier**: Contributors can still use `docker build .` without learning bake

## Files Changed

- `docker/versions.json` - Bake-native version manifest (auto-generated)
- `docker/Dockerfile` - Source of truth for version defaults
- `tools/generate_versions_json.py` - Auto-generation script with --check mode

## Test Plan

- [x] JSON is valid and parseable by bake
- [x] `docker buildx bake --print` shows correct variable values
- [x] `python tools/generate_versions_json.py --check` passes
- [x] Pre-commit checks pass

issue.title: [CI/Build] Fix ffmpeg CVEs
issue.body:
## Purpose

The current vLLM Docker images contain **28 security vulnerabilities** in FFmpeg and related libraries, including **3 Critical** and **11 High** severity CVEs. These vulnerabilities stem from using Ubuntu's universe repository version of FFmpeg (4.4.2), which requires Ubuntu Pro subscription for security patches.

The vulnerabilities exist because:

1. **Ubuntu Universe Repository**: vLLM uses FFmpeg from Ubuntu's universe repository
2. **No Free Security Updates**: Ubuntu universe packages require **Ubuntu Pro subscription** for security patches
3. **Outdated Version**: FFmpeg 4.4.2 has known vulnerabilities that remain unpatched in the free Ubuntu repositories

### Affected Files:
- Any Dockerfiles in the repository that install FFmpeg from Ubuntu

### Impact

#### Vulnerability Breakdown
- **Critical Severity**: 3 CVEs
- **High Severity**: 11 CVEs  
- **Medium Severity**: 14 CVEs
- **Total**: 28 unique CVEs

#### Affected Components
All FFmpeg-related libraries in vLLM Docker images (version 4.4.2):
- `ffmpeg`
- `libavcodec58`, `libavdevice58`, `libavfilter7`, `libavformat58`
- `libavresample4`, `libavutil56`, `libpostproc55`
- `libswresample3`, `libswscale5`

### Critical CVEs (Immediate Action Required)

| CVE ID | Severity | Component | Reference |
|--------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| CVE-2024-35366 | **CRITICAL** | ffmpeg 4.4.2 | [Ubuntu Security](https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-35366.html) |
| CVE-2024-35367 | **CRITICAL** | ffmpeg 4.4.2 | [Ubuntu Security](https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-35367.html) |
| CVE-2024-35368 | **CRITICAL** | ffmpeg 4.4.2 | [Ubuntu Security](https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-35368.html) |

### CVE Summary

<details>
<summary>Click to expand CVE Summary </summary>

## CVE Summary

| CVE ID | Severity | Reference URL |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| CVE-2024-35368 | CRITICAL | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-35368.html |
| CVE-2024-35366 | CRITICAL | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-35366.html |
| CVE-2024-35367 | CRITICAL | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-35367.html |
| CVE-2023-6603 | HIGH | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2023/CVE-2023-6603.html |
| CVE-2023-51793 | HIGH | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2023/CVE-2023-51793.html |
| CVE-2022-3109 | HIGH | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2022/CVE-2022-3109.html |

| CVE-2023-50010 | HIGH | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2023/CVE-2023-50010.html |
| CVE-2022-48434 | HIGH | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2022/CVE-2022-48434.html |
| CVE-2023-6605 | HIGH | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2023/CVE-2023-6605.html |
| CVE-2024-32230 | HIGH | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-32230.html |
| CVE-2024-31578 | HIGH | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-31578.html |
| CVE-2023-51794 | HIGH | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2023/CVE-2023-51794.html |
| CVE-2023-49502 | HIGH | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2023/CVE-2023-49502.html |
| CVE-2025-10256 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2025/CVE-2025-10256.html |
| CVE-2025-0518 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2025/CVE-2025-0518.html |
| CVE-2024-36616 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-36616.html |
| CVE-2025-1594 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2025/CVE-2025-1594.html |
| CVE-2022-3341 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2022/CVE-2022-3341.html |
| CVE-2024-36617 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-36617.html |
| CVE-2025-7700 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2025/CVE-2025-7700.html |
| CVE-2022-3964 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2022/CVE-2022-3964.html |
| CVE-2024-7055 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-7055.html |
| CVE-2024-36613 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-36613.html |
| CVE-2025-22919 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2025/CVE-2025-22919.html |
| CVE-2025-22921 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2025/CVE-2025-22921.html |
| CVE-2024-36618 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2024/CVE-2024-36618.html |
| CVE-2025-9951 | MEDIUM | https://people.canonical.com/~ubuntu-security/cve/2025/CVE-2025-9951.html |

</details>

### Solution:
Replace Ubuntu's vulnerable FFmpeg 4.4.2 with security-patched Debian Bullseye version 4.3.9-0+deb11u1 to eliminate all 28 CVEs without requiring Ubuntu Pro subscription.

## Test Plan
CI Test
- https://buildkite.com/vllm/ci/builds/47260

## Test Result
Pass, failed `v1-test-attention-b200` also fail on main.

</details>

---

> [!NOTE]
> Pins secure Debian Bullseye `ffmpeg` and related libraries in the `vllm-base` Docker stage to mitigate vulnerabilities.
> 
> - Adds `gnupg`, configures Debian Bullseye repos and keyrings, and installs pinned `ffmpeg=7:4.3.9-0+deb11u1` with associated `libav*` and `libchromaprint1`
> - Removes prior unpinned Ubuntu `ffmpeg` install; cleans up Bullseye sources/keyrings post-install to keep image lean
> - No other build stages or application code changed
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 64d627662b965d827263aa6ebe15a93b9b3d565d. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>

---

> [!NOTE]
> Addresses FFmpeg CVEs in the runtime Docker image by switching to Debian Bullseye’s security-patched packages with strict pinning.
> 
> - In `vllm-base` stage, add `gnupg`, configure Debian Bullseye keyrings/sources, and install pinned `ffmpeg` and related `libav*` packages (`4.3.9-0+deb11u1`) plus `libchromaprint1`
> - Remove prior unpinned `ffmpeg` install; delete temporary Bullseye sources and keys after install to keep image clean
> - No functional changes to build/test flows beyond Dockerfile adjustments
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 64d627662b965d827263aa6ebe15a93b9b3d565d. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>

CGO_ENABLED='1'
CGO_FFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CGO_LDFLAGS='-O2 -g'
CXX='g++'
GCCGO='gccgo'
GO111MODULE=''
GOAMD64='v1'
GOARCH='amd64'
GOAUTH='netrc'
GOBIN=''
GOCACHE='/root/.cache/go-build'
GOCACHEPROG=''
GODEBUG=''
GOENV='/root/.config/go/env'
GOEXE=''
GOEXPERIMENT=''
GOFIPS140='off'
GOFLAGS='-buildvcs=false'
GOGCCFLAGS='-fPIC -m64 -pthread -Wl,--no-gc-sections -fmessage-length=0 -ffile-prefix-map=/tmp/go-build168762594=/tmp/go-build -gno-record-gcc-switches'
GOHOSTARCH='amd64'
GOHOSTOS='linux'
GOINSECURE=''
GOMOD='/code/go.mod'
GOMODCACHE='/go/pkg/mod'
GONOPROXY='git.synesis.ru'
GONOSUMDB='git.synesis.ru'
GOOS='linux'
GOPATH='/go'
GOPRIVATE='git.synesis.ru'
GOPROXY='https://proxy.golang.org,direct'
GOROOT='/usr/local/go'
GOSUMDB='sum.golang.org'
GOTELEMETRY='local'
GOTELEMETRYDIR='/root/.config/go/telemetry'
GOTMPDIR=''
GOTOOLCHAIN='local'
GOTOOLDIR='/usr/local/go/pkg/tool/linux_amd64'
GOVCS=''
GOVERSION='go1.25.6'
GOWORK=''
PKG_CONFIG='pkg-config'
```

### What did you do?

## Issue Description
After upgrading from Go 1.25.5 to Go 1.25.6, our CGO-based code that uses pkg-config with the `--define-variable` flag no longer compiles. The error occurs during the type checking phase.

## Error Message
cmd/gochannel/va/audio/aa/event.go:1: : invalid flag in pkg-config --cflags: --define-variable=prefix=/usr/lib/va/audioanalytics (typecheck)

## Affected Code
The issue occurs in a Go file that includes C library bindings:
```
package aa
// #cgo pkg-config: --define-variable=prefix=/usr/lib/va/audioanalytics audioanalytics
// #include "aa/AudioAnalytics.h"
// extern void audio_logger_go(char* message, void* user_data);
import "C"
```

## Workaround
Setting the environment variable CGO_CFLAGS_ALLOW='.*define.*variable.*' resolves the compilation issue:

```
export CGO_CFLAGS_ALLOW='.*define.*variable.*'
```

## pkg-config
The audioanalytics.pc file contains:

```
prefix=/code/src/AudioAnalytics/AudioAnalyticsClt/build/install
library_dir=libs
exec_prefix=${prefix}
includedir=${prefix}/include
libdir=${prefix}/${library_dir}
Name: AudioAnalytics
Description: The AudioAnalytics library
Version: 1.0.7
Libs: -L${libdir} -lAudioAnalyticsLib -ldl -lz -lm -lstdc++
Cflags: -I${includedir}
```

## Questions?

- Has --define-variable become a disallowed flag in Go 1.25.6? 

- Is this an intentional security restriction, or a regression in flag validation?

- If --define-variable is now prohibited, what is the recommended approach for overriding pkg-config variables in #cgo pkg-config: directives?

- Should we file this against the pkg-config tool instead, or is this a Go CGO validation issue?

### What did you see happen?

## Error Message
cmd/gochannel/va/audio/aa/event.go:1: : invalid flag in pkg-config --cflags: --define-variable=prefix=/usr/lib/va/audioanalytics (typecheck)

### What did you expect to see?

Successful build

comment.body: Actually in retrospect I think I went overboard. C code can already refer to any directory via `#include`. Probably `--define-variable` is safe as long as the argument doesn't include any whitespace, commas, and so forth. Actually I'm not sure which `pkg-config` flags are unsafe. I must be missing something.

issue.title: proposal: runtime: expose setPinned

labels.name: LibraryProposal
labels.description: Issues describing a requested change to the Go standard library or x/ libraries, but not to a tool

milestone.title: Proposal
milestone.description:
Proposals that are pending (not yet accepted/rejected).
See https://go.dev/s/proposal-process.

issue.body:
### Proposal Details

Right now, cgo requires that Go pointers are pinned by `runtime.Pinner` for the duration that C code has access to them. Pinner itself has a [finalizer](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/refs/tags/go1.25.6:src/runtime/pinner.go;l=51) that requires that when the Pinner is finalized it is empty in the interest of preventing leaks. When binding callback-oriented C code where we pass a Go callback that is called at some point in the future (e.g. upon completion of I/O) the binding library must ensure that the Pinner that pins the callback's data remains reachable or else the callback will segfault when it is called.

Since library code cannot ensure that the user code will maintain such a reference, the library code must keep the Pinner reachable from a global variable, likely either through creating a new goroutine to reference it or using a global `sync.Map`. The `sync.Map` approach seems redundant with just storing the callback's data in the map instead (a la [`runtime/cgo.Handle`](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/go1.25.6:src/runtime/cgo/handle.go;l=114)), and the goroutine approach seems overly expensive when done repeatedly.

Bypassing `runtime.Pinner` and using `//go:linkname` to directly call [`runtime.setPinned`](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/refs/tags/go1.25.6:src/runtime/pinner.go;l=149) works since the callback can unpin its own data, so in the spirit of removing dependencies on `//go:linkname` it felt relevant to propose exposing it.

comment.body:
That's fair.

How do you know when to unpin your pointer? Presumably you have the pointer somewhere, and you know when you are done with it. Can you just use a `Pinner` instead? Or a struct with a `Pinner` and your pointer? Pin a pointer to that struct in the `Pinner`. Then the `Pinner` will keep itself alive. When done, call `Unpin` and drop all references to the struct.

issue.title: Forking process doesn't give errors in simple program, but seems to deadlock NGINX when loaded as shared library

labels.name: Question
labels.description: Issues that are questions about using Go.

comment.body: I don't know enough about nginx to know whether there is some way that nginx can use a plugin written in Go. But I can confirm that using `fork` with Go code can't work. Any approach that requires nginx to `dlopen` and call into a Go c-shared library, and then call `fork`, will fail. Sure, some things will work some of the time. But there will definitely be failures, notably around garbage collection and scheduling around blocking system calls. This isn't all that specific to Go. Using `fork` with a multi-threaded program is inherently unsafe, unless that multi-threaded program is carefully written to work with `fork`. Go is not.

### What did you do?

## Issue Description

## Error Message
cmd/gochannel/va/audio/aa/event.go:1: : invalid flag in pkg-config --cflags: --define-variable=prefix=/usr/lib/va/audioanalytics (typecheck)

## Affected Code

```

```

## pkg-config

```
prefix=/code/src/AudioAnalytics/AudioAnalyticsClt/build/install

Name: AudioAnalytics

## Questions?

- Should we file this against the pkg-config tool instead, or is this a Go CGO validation issue?

### What did you see happen?

## Error Message
cmd/gochannel/va/audio/aa/event.go:1: : invalid flag in pkg-config --cflags: --define-variable=prefix=/usr/lib/va/audioanalytics (typecheck)

### What did you expect to see?

Successful build

comment.body:
I don't understand the change that caused this ([https://go.dev/cl/736711](https://go.dev/cl/736711)). That change appears to be validating the list of flags passed to `pkg-config` against the list of flags that are permitted for the C compiler. That does not make sense. The set of flags that `pkg-config` accepts has nothing to do with the set of flags that the C compiler accepts. If we want to limit `pkg-config` to a safe set of flags, we need a list of flags for `pkg-config`. Using the list of flags for the C compiler isn't right.

That said, if we maintain a list of flags that are safe to use with `pkg-config`, `--define-variable` would not be on that list. `--define-variable` permits redirecting the flags from `pkg-config` to more or less anything. I can imagine that somebody could use that to collect information from surprising system directories. That information would then be compiled into the code, and perhaps used in some inappropriate way.

So even if I am right that [CL 736711](https://go.dev/cl/736711) needs correcting, I think your specific case is correctly rejected, and the environment variable override, either something like `CGO_PKG_CONFIG_ALLOW` or the existing `PKG_CONFIG`, is still going to be required. In general it's never going to be appropriate for Go code downloaded from the Internet to refer to arbitrary directories on the build system, which is exactly what your `--define-variable` option is doing.

CC @thatnealpatel @golang/security

issue.title: time: memory leak when repeatedly reassigning time.Local with results from LoadLocation/LoadLocationFromTZData

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
### Go version

Go Version: go1.24.1 linux/amd64

### Output of `go env` in your module/workspace:

```shell
any
```

### What did you do?

There is a subtle memory leak pattern when `time.Local` is updated dynamically. When `time.LoadLocation` or `time.LoadLocationFromTZData` is called, it returns a pointer to a **newly allocated** `time.Location` object. 

If a program repeatedly updates the global `time.Local` with these new instances (e.g., in a scheduled task or dynamic configuration update), any `time.Time` object created via `time.Now()` (or any constructor using `Local`) will hold a reference to that specific `Location` instance.

If these `time.Time` objects are long-lived (e.g., stored in a global cache, map, or persistent buffer), they prevent the specific `Location` instance from being garbage collected. Over time, as `time.Local` is reassigned again and again, the number of `Location` objects pinned in memory grows linearly, leading to a significant memory leak.

**Reproduction:**
The following code demonstrates that even after `time.Local` is changed to a new value, the previous `Location` objects remain in memory because they are referenced by `time.Time` objects created during that "era".

https://go.dev/play/p/mJ9CCQ150yW

### What did you see happen?

The location pointer cannot be reclaimed, causing memory usage to continuously increase.

### What did you expect to see?

While users can avoid this by:
1. Not reassigning `time.Local` and using `time.In(loc)` instead.
2. Caching `Location` objects and reusing them.

The fact that `time.Local` is a public exported variable suggests it can be changed. However, the current implementation of `time.Time` makes it a "reference trap".

Ideally, if two `Location` objects are identical in content (same name, offsets, and transition rules), `time.Time` objects should perhaps not treat them as distinct roots for GC, or `LoadLocation` should have a built-in intern mechanism. At the very least, the documentation for `time.Local` should explicitly warn against frequent reassignments with newly loaded locations.

comment.body: This seems like a problem that relatively few programs will encounter, and, as you say, those programs can use their own cache to avoid the problem. Should we just address this with documentation?

issue.title: proposal: debug/pe: provide list of exported symbols for DLLs

labels.name: LibraryProposal
labels.description: Issues describing a requested change to the Go standard library or x/ libraries, but not to a tool

milestone.title: Proposal
milestone.description:
Proposals that are pending (not yet accepted/rejected).
See https://go.dev/s/proposal-process.

issue.body:
### Proposal Details

Right now, debug/pe doesn't support returning the list of exported symbols. I have currently my own code to do that; needed the following:

- A helper to read a byte range in form of relative virtual addresses (might make sense to have this on pe.File?)
  - should note that although this _usually_ is in the `.edata` section, it can technically be anywhere, and thus it's best to not rely on section names for this at all; so instead one would iterate through all section to find the one that includes the desired byte range, and read from there
- A helper to read a 0-terminated string from a relative virtual address (ditto)
- get export directory address from data directory (already present in debug/pe)
- read the export directory
- parse the export directory into a struct (needed the name pointers count and the address of the name pointers)
- read the name pointers into a slice
- for every name pointer, read a 0-terminated string from that address

While at it, I would actually suggest implementing the full DLL export directory (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/debug/pe-format#the-edata-section-image-only), which then can't just output the names of the exports, but also yield a full list of `[]struct{VirtualAddress uint32; Name string}` indexed by the DLL symbol's ordinal.

Is this feature generally desired? I could offer implementing it and sending it for review.

I will likely need to include some trivial mingw-w64-compiled DLLs as test cases though - would that be actually OK?

comment.body:
@divVerent It would help if you could write down the new exported types, methods, and functions that you suggest we add to debug/pe, along with their documentation. Thanks.

Note that we aren't very likely to add an exported function to read a zero-terminated string. But accessors for the export directory is likely fine.

issue.title: proposal: spec: generic methods for Go

labels.name: LanguageChange
labels.description: Suggested changes to the Go language

labels.name: generics
labels.description: Issue is related to generics

milestone.title: Proposal
milestone.description:
Proposals that are pending (not yet accepted/rejected).
See https://go.dev/s/proposal-process.

issue.body:
# Proposal: Generic Methods for Go

## A change of view.

## Background

For clarity, in the following we use the term *concrete method* (or just method when the context is clear) to describe a *non-interface method* declared like a function but with a receiver; and we use the term *interface method* to describe the name and signature of a *method of an [interface](https://golang.org/ref/spec#Interface_types)*.

Per the current [spec](https://golang.org/ref/spec#Method_declarations), a concrete method is a function with a receiver. Syntactically this is not quite true: while functions can be generic, methods cannot. They cannot declare new type parameters themselves; but they can have a receiver of a generic type (and thus have method-local names for the type parameters of the receiver type).

A reason for this discrepancy is that we have historically viewed the primary role of methods as a means to implement an [interface](https://golang.org/ref/spec#Interface_types): permitting type parameters on concrete methods would imply that we must also permit type parameters on interface methods. Go doesn't support such generic interface methods because we don't know how to implement (calls of) them, or at least we don't know how to implement them efficiently. Specifically, because Go doesn't require a concrete type to declare the interfaces it implements, and instead this is a dynamic property, it cannot be known at compile time which of the infinite possible instantiations of concrete methods will be needed at run time. This shortcoming has long been known and was [discussed at length](https://go.googlesource.com/proposal/+/refs/heads/master/design/43651-type-parameters.md#No-parameterized-methods) in the original [Type Parameters Proposal](https://go.googlesource.com/proposal/+/refs/heads/master/design/43651-type-parameters.md).

But concrete methods are not just a means for implementing interfaces. A method is a function associated with a type, and accessed through the namespace of that type. Therefore methods are useful for organizing code even if they don't ever implement an interface. Furthermore there is a syntactic benefit: x.a().b().c() may naturally be read left to right, whereas c(b(a(x))) is evaluated inside out. Both these aspects of methods are also well known.

For these reasons, Go users have requested generic methods for a long time and the idea is widely popular. There are at least two proposals filed on the issue tracker:

* [\#49085](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/49085) (Allow type parameters in methods, Oct. 2021, with \> 900 positive emojis)  
* [\#50981](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/50981) (Add generics to methods, Feb. 2022\)

So far we have resisted adding generic concrete methods because it always implied that we also needed generic interface methods. The [Go FAQ](https://go.dev/doc/faq#generic_methods) even states that "we do not anticipate that Go will ever add generic methods". Perhaps a change of view is in order: concrete methods are a language feature that is useful in itself, irrespective of interfaces. If a concrete method is a function with a receiver, a generic concrete method can be a generic function with a receiver. The fact that such methods may not be invoked via an interface is an orthogonal aspect: if an interface syntactically can’t include a method with type parameters, then a generic concrete method naturally plays no role in satisfying that interface because there can’t be an interface method with matching type parameters.

Note that the original Type Parameters Proposal discussed [this alternative view](https://go.googlesource.com/proposal/+/refs/heads/master/design/43651-type-parameters.md#No-parameterized-methods) as well, as expressed in the following paragraph:

"Or, we could decide that parameterized methods do not, in fact, implement interfaces, but then it's much less clear why we need methods at all. If we disregard interfaces, any parameterized method can be implemented as a parameterized function."

We may have reached some clarity on this point: generic concrete methods are useful by themselves, even if they don't implement interface methods.

## Proposal

We propose that concrete method declarations should look exactly like function declarations, but with receivers. Specifically, the syntax of a method declaration should accept type parameters as do function declarations. The syntax of function declarations is as follows:

```ebnf
FunctionDecl = "func" FunctionName [ TypeParameters ] Signature [ FunctionBody ] .
```

The syntax of method declarations should be changed from (old):

```ebnf
MethodDecl = "func" Receiver MethodName Signature [ FunctionBody ] .
```

to (new):

```ebnf
MethodDecl = "func" Receiver MethodName [ TypeParameters ] Signature [ FunctionBody ] .
```

Alternatively, to the same effect, the syntax can express directly what we say in prose: a method (declaration) is a function (declaration) with a receiver, and combine the two productions. This removes a syntax production and emphasizes the similarities:

```ebnf
FunctionDecl = "func" [ Receiver ] identifier [ TypeParameters ] Signature [ FunctionBody ] .
```

The scope of an identifier denoting a type parameter of a generic method begins after the name of the method and ends at the end of the method body. This matches existing rules for type parameters of functions and method receivers. Constraints for method type parameters may refer to type parameters declared with the method receiver because they are in scope.

Calling a generic concrete method works as expected: either, type arguments are provided as needed, or type inference determines any missing type arguments, exactly like for generic function calls.

[Method expressions](https://golang.org/ref/spec#Method_expressions) and [method values](https://golang.org/ref/spec#Method_values) continue to work as expected: if the method is generic, the resulting function is generic and the existing rules for using generic functions apply.

The grammar will need a small adjustment: currently type arguments may only be supplied to operand names which are (possibly package-qualified) identifiers for generic functions and types. Per the [syntax for operands](https://golang.org/ref/spec#Operands) (old):

```ebnf
Operand     = Literal | OperandName [ TypeArgs ] | "(" Expression ")" .  
…  
OperandName = identifier | QualifiedIdent .  
TypeArgs    = "[" TypeList [ "," ] "]" .
```

Methods can be attached to arbitrary user-defined types, and values of such types may be denoted by various expressions, not just qualified identifiers. To permit instantiation of generic methods, type arguments must move from the syntax for operands to the [syntax for primary expressions](https://golang.org/ref/spec#Primary_expressions), resulting in the updated productions (new):

```ebnf
Operand     = Literal | OperandName | "(" Expression ")" .

PrimaryExpr = Operand |
              Conversion |
              MethodExpr |
              PrimaryExpr Selector |
              PrimaryExpr Index |
              PrimaryExpr Slice |
              PrimaryExpr TypeAssertion |
              PrimaryExpr Arguments |
              PrimaryExpr TypeArgs .
```

(Notably, type arguments are already parsed as part of primary expressions in the current implementation. This is necessary because an instantiation such as T\[int\] and an index expression a\[i\] are syntactically indistinguishable. In the language specification, the syntactic overlap between these two is maintained solely for the sake of documentation.)

Methods of interfaces are not changed. Importantly, a generic concrete method does not match against an interface method with the same name and signature because the interface method syntactically cannot have matching type parameters.

Generic methods also won't be accessible via reflection (by name or index) for the same reason that uninstantiated generic functions are not accessible: the reflect package doesn't have a mechanism to instantiate a generic value or type (and it is unclear how one would provide such a mechanism).

This is the entirety of the proposal.

## Examples

Given a (non-interface) receiver base type S, we may associate a generic method m with it:

```go
type S struct { … }  
func (*S) m[P any](x P) { … }
```

Given a variable s of type S (or a value of type \*S), we can call m as follows:

```go
var s S  
s.m[int](42)    // explicit type argument int  
s.m(x)          // type argument P is inferred from x
```

A receiver base type may itself be generic:

```go
type G[P any] struct{ … }  
func (*G[P]) m[Q any](x Q) { … }
```

Calling m requires a variable whose type is an instantiation of G, but there is no other difference in the way m is called.

An interface I with a method m:

```go
type I interface {  
	m(string)  
}
```
may be implemented by a suitably instantiated generic type G (this is true in current Go):

```go
type G[P any] struct{ … }  
func (G[P]) m(P) { … }

var g G[string]  
var _ I = g		// valid because G[string].m has signature m(string) which matches m(string) of I
```

But I is not implemented by a type H with a generic method m:

```go
type H struct{ … }  
func (H) m[P any](P) { … }

var h H  
var _ I = h		// invalid because H.m has signature m[P any](P) which doesn't match m(string) of I
```

Here's another, less abstract example, related to the Go library. A Reader type with a generic Read method:

```go
type Reader struct{ … }  
func (*Reader) Read[E any]([]E) (int, error) { … }
```

does not implement io.Reader, even though it might if there were some way to instantiate the method as (\*Reader).Read\[byte\] (which there is not, and we are not proposing it).

Method expressions (and method values) work as expected. Given the Reader type from above, the method expression Reader.Read produces a generic function with type parameters and signature:

```go
[E any](*Reader, []E) (int, error)
```

If the type itself is generic, it must be instantiated (per existing Go [rules for generic types](https://golang.org/ref/spec#Type_declarations)) before it can be used in a method expression. For instance, given a generic List type with a generic formatting method format:

```go
type List[E any] struct { … }  
func (*List[E]) format[F any](E, F) string { … }
```

the method expression List\[string\].format will result in a function with type parameters and signature

```go
[F any](string, F) string
```

In particular, List.format is not a valid method expression (List is not instantiated), nor does it produce the generic function signature

```go
[E any, F any](E, F) string	// not the signature of List.format
```

## Implementation

### Specification

The change to the language specification is small and fully backward-compatible. The syntax changes have been outlined above. The corresponding prose changes are similarly straightforward.

Whenever there is a language change, updating the specification is a tiny part of the work needed to get documentation updated in general. This includes existing documents traditionally maintained by the Go team (which we plan to keep fresh as much as possible), to 3P documentation beyond our control (external blog post, articles, books, etc.). These will become partly out of date and may or may not be updated, but that is not avoidable if we want to make changes to the language.

### Compiler

The parser already accepts type parameters for methods for robustness but complains with an error. Removing that error is a trivial change. The parser also already handles index expressions and instantiations together. If any changes are needed at all, they will be minimal. The type checker will need various adjustments, most likely involving the removal of restrictions, the details of which will only become apparent by doing the actual work.

The compiler back-end changes are likely more significant. That said, method calls via non-interface receivers can be resolved statically (at compile time): because the type of the receiver is not an interface, its type is statically known, and thus the called method is also statically known. Conceptually, such method calls can be rewritten into function calls. If the method itself is generic, the method call can be rewritten into a call of a corresponding generic function.

For instance, given the type G, method m, and instance x of G:

```go
type G[P any] struct{ … }  
func (G[P]) m[Q any](x Q) { … }

var g G[string]
```

the method call

```go
var s string  
g.m(s)
```

can be translated into a function call f(s) of the generic function

```go
func f[Q any](g G[string], x Q) {  
	G[string].m[Q](g, x)		// using a call of method expression G[string].m  
}
```

or

```go
func f[Q any](g G[string], x Q) {  
	g.m[Q](x)			// using a call of method value g.m[Q]  
}
```

This is not to say that an implementation will be trivial. But the principal translation mechanism is understood.

The import/export data format will need to be updated to allow for type parameters on methods. This is likely the most disruptive change because of the many different exporters and importers that exist, some of which are used for language tools, reside in different repositories, and all must remain in sync with the compiler's export format. Carefully staged updates are needed in order to not break existing infrastructure. We have done import/export data format changes in the past, and while tedious, they can be done.

### Libraries and tools

No change of the existing libraries is required, but future (versions of these) libraries may want to use generic methods.

The impact on tools may be significant as they will need to handle the new language feature. The [go/types](https://pkg.go.dev/go/types) API's [Signature type](https://pkg.go.dev/go/types#Signature) already provides accessors for receiver and ordinary type parameters, so it's possible that no API changes are needed there, but [go/types](https://pkg.go.dev/go/types) clients cannot rely on the (current) fact that only one of type parameter lists is present. Judging from past experience, it may take one or two release cycles for all tools to catch up. It is difficult to judge the amount work involved without actually doing the work.

## Other considerations

This proposal adds a new feature to the language by removing a restriction; thus it is fully backward-compatible with existing Go. Importantly, it also doesn't preclude the implementation of generic interface methods at some point, should we find an acceptable implementation solution that doesn't impose a cost when the feature is not used.

Generic functions and types make it possible to write more complex code and adding generic methods will further increase that capability. But used appropriately, generic methods can simplify and generalize situations where currently more complex work-arounds are needed (see [\#49085](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/49085) for an example).

comment.body:
> I see this example, but I also see an example of very bad code. Parameterized interfaces are designed to save us from any use `any`.

I don't agree. Generics have their uses. Interfaces, including `any`, have their uses. I wrote about it at https://go.dev/blog/when-generics.

In any case, this seems off-topic here.

issue.title: cmd/link: relocation addend overflow

labels.name: Soon
labels.description: This needs action soon. (recent regressions, service outages, unusual time-sensitive situations)

labels.name: compiler/runtime
labels.description: Issues related to the Go compiler and/or runtime.

labels.name: FixPending
labels.description: Issues that have a fix which has not yet been reviewed or submitted.

labels.name: BugReport
labels.description: Issues describing a possible bug in the Go implementation.

issue.body:
@ianlancetaylor

After [CL 724261](https://go.dev/cl/724261), we've started getting some errors on some Google internal builds. The errors are on darwin/arm64 external linking, and look like:

```
runtime.typelinksinit: internal error: relocation addend overflow: type:*+0x-9131d0
```

for lots of different symbols.

So the offset is both big and negative. The code we have to handle large offsets doesn't deal with large negative offsets correctly.

So that would need to be fixed somehow. I suspect the real problem is negative offsets of any size. We get those even in normal builds after 724261 (if you log what is going on in one of the locations linked below, it will print negative `xadd` values after 724261 but not before). I'm not sure what a negative offset means, exactly. Seems to work though? At least as long as the offset is not too big. If the negative offset means we're inside a larger symbol, then maybe we could use the containing symbol's label symbols instead?

(To handle large offsets into a symbol, we [add a new virtual label symbols every 8MB(?)](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/baa6b1f2bf258a857dedddf8be9cb1779bad797a/src/cmd/link/internal/arm64/asm.go#L1282) and [redirect relocations to use those label symbols](https://github.com/golang/go/blob/baa6b1f2bf258a857dedddf8be9cb1779bad797a/src/cmd/link/internal/arm64/asm.go#L598), so the offset recorded in the relocation is small enough.)

comment.body: @randall77 Can you see whether https://go.dev/cl/740400 fixes the problem?
